title: Address initial code review findings
description:
## Outcome
Track and resolve the initial 2026-04-07 code review findings so the Njalla webhook is safe under concurrency, reports reconciliation failures correctly, enforces its domain boundary, handles unmanaged-zone discovery correctly, and ships deployment artifacts that match the runtime API.

## Why this exists
The first full repository audit found several defects that can break production correctness or operator confidence even though the crate builds cleanly. This epic keeps those fixes grouped as a review-remediation batch instead of leaving them as informal notes.

## Scope
Create and drive the five concrete bug-fix tasks from the audit.

## Non-goals
Do not add unrelated features or broad refactors that are not required to close the review findings.

## Key implementation notes
Each child bead should preserve external-dns webhook compatibility, include regression coverage for the failure mode it fixes, and update docs or deployment assets only where the bug requires it.

## Dependencies / ordering
This epic is the parent for the audit tasks. Most children can proceed in parallel; only add task-to-task dependencies where shared matching logic or sequencing is real.

## Success Criteria
- All five review findings are represented by actionable child beads with clear ownership boundaries.
- The graph stays acyclic and leaves a useful ready frontier for implementation.
- Closing the child beads is sufficient to resolve the initial review batch without reopening the original audit just to recover intent.

## Verification
- Unit tests: Child beads add focused regression coverage.
- Integration or e2e tests: Mocked Njalla/external-dns flows are added where behavior crosses process boundaries.
- Logging / observability / diagnostics: Error paths stay visible without leaking secrets.
- Acceptance signal: All child beads are closed and the project passes `cargo test` and `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`.

## Spec refs
- Code review findings from 2026-04-07 across `src/njalla/types.rs`, `src/webhook/handlers.rs`, `src/config.rs`, and `flake.nix`.

title: Replace unsafe JSON-RPC request ID generation
description:
## Outcome
`JsonRpcRequest::new` generates JSON-RPC request IDs without unsynchronized global mutation, so concurrent Njalla API calls are memory-safe and keep stable wire behavior under load.

## Why this exists
The current implementation uses `static mut` in async server code. That is undefined behavior in Rust when multiple requests race through the client, and it can corrupt request IDs or worse.

## Scope
Replace the unsafe global counter with a concurrency-safe ID generator, keep the request ID format compatible with the Njalla API, and add regression coverage for repeated or parallel ID generation.

## Non-goals
Do not redesign the Njalla client interface or change unrelated request/response types.

## Key implementation notes
The bug is in `src/njalla/types.rs`. Prefer a safe monotonic generator such as `AtomicU32` unless the API contract forces something else. Decide how wraparound should behave and document the choice if it is not obvious from the code.

## Dependencies / ordering
No blockers. This can be implemented independently of the other audit fixes.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run the webhook under concurrent request load so multiple Njalla API calls can construct JSON-RPC requests at the same time.
2. Inspect `JsonRpcRequest::new` and note that request IDs come from shared `static mut` state without synchronization.
3. Observe that this is undefined behavior in Rust regardless of whether a duplicate ID is reproduced in a small manual test.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `JsonRpcRequest::new` no longer uses `static mut` or any other unsynchronized mutable global state.
- Request ID generation remains valid for the Njalla JSON-RPC API and is safe under concurrent access.
- Regression coverage exercises repeated and parallel request construction.

## Verification
- Unit tests: Generate IDs from repeated and parallel call sites and confirm the tested sample does not duplicate or race.
- Integration or e2e tests: Existing client serialization still produces valid JSON-RPC requests.
- Logging / observability / diagnostics: No extra noisy logs; add a short code comment only if the generator behavior is non-obvious.
- Acceptance signal: `cargo test` and `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` pass, and this path no longer uses `static mut`.

## Spec refs
- Review finding from 2026-04-07 against `src/njalla/types.rs:74`.

title: Make apply_changes failure semantics safe for reconciliation
description:
## Outcome
The `/records` apply path reports failure accurately and does not silently acknowledge partial or destructive update failures during external-dns reconciliation.

## Why this exists
After PR #5 the handler returns `204 No Content` for all non-total-failure cases. When at least one operation succeeds but others fail, the response is still 204 — which tells external-dns the sync succeeded when DNS state is actually incomplete. Updates are still delete-then-create, so a failed create after a successful delete leaves records missing with a success signal.

## Scope
Rework `apply_changes` in `src/webhook/handlers.rs` so partial failures return a non-success status code (e.g. 500 or 207), letting the reconciler retry. Add regression coverage for mixed create/update/delete batches and for create failures after delete-on-update.

## Non-goals
Do not build a large transactional framework beyond what is needed to make reconciliation safe and observable.

## Key implementation notes
The fix must account for the batch-level response contract, not just per-record logging. If delete-then-create remains necessary, the implementation must still surface the failure clearly and avoid pretending the sync succeeded. Prefer behavior that allows the next reconcile loop to recover deterministically. Note: `ApplyChangesResponse` was removed in PR #5 — the handler now returns `StatusCode` directly.

## Dependencies / ordering
Independent of the other audit tasks.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Submit a `/records` change set that contains at least one successful operation and one failing operation.
2. Include an update case where deleting the old record succeeds but creating the replacement fails.
3. Observe that the handler still returns 204 No Content even though DNS state is incomplete.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Partial or destructive failures return a non-success HTTP status rather than 204.
- Update behavior is safe enough that the next reconcile loop can recover deterministically.
- Regression coverage exists for mixed batches and delete-then-create failure paths.

## Verification
- Unit tests: Cover empty changes, all-success batches, partial-failure batches, and mismatched update-old/update-new edge cases if applicable.
- Integration or e2e tests: Use mocked Njalla calls to simulate create failures after delete and confirm the handler returns a non-success status code.
- Logging / observability / diagnostics: Failed operations remain visible in logs without returning a false-positive 204.
- Acceptance signal: A simulated partial failure no longer results in a 204 webhook response.

## Spec refs
- Review findings from 2026-04-07 against `src/webhook/handlers.rs:220` and `src/webhook/handlers.rs:285`.

comments.author: master
comments.text: PR #5 (d53fa42) changed apply_changes to return StatusCode::NO_CONTENT and removed ApplyChangesResponse. The core issue remains: partial failures still return 204. Updated description to reflect current code state.

title: Enforce exact domain-filter boundaries
description:
## Outcome
Configured domain filters allow only the exact managed zone or true subdomains of that zone, preventing sibling-domain escapes such as `badexample.com` matching `example.com`.

## Why this exists
The current `ends_with` check in `Config::is_domain_allowed` is too loose for DNS boundaries. That weakens the project’s main safety control for limiting which domains the webhook may touch.

## Scope
Tighten the matching logic in `src/config.rs` to enforce exact-zone or `.<zone>` suffix matching, normalize inputs as needed for DNS comparisons, and add regression tests for allowed and rejected names.

## Non-goals
Do not add regex-based filtering or redesign the configuration format.

## Key implementation notes
The fix should consider common DNS normalization issues such as case and optional trailing dots if those can appear in webhook input. The important invariant is that sibling domains never pass the filter by string accident.

## Dependencies / ordering
This is independent, but its matching helper should be reusable by any zone-discovery logic added elsewhere.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Configure `DOMAIN_FILTER=example.com`.
2. Call code paths that check whether `example.com`, `www.example.com`, and `badexample.com` are allowed.
3. Observe that the current string-suffix check incorrectly accepts the sibling domain.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The matcher allows the exact zone and proper subdomains only.
- Sibling domains that merely end with the same characters are rejected.
- Regression tests cover accepted and rejected examples, including any supported normalization rules.

## Verification
- Unit tests: Exact zone match, subdomain match, sibling-domain rejection, and any supported normalization cases.
- Integration or e2e tests: Requests for disallowed zones fail before Njalla mutations are attempted.
- Logging / observability / diagnostics: Rejected domains remain clearly attributable in logs and error responses.
- Acceptance signal: `badexample.com` no longer passes a filter for `example.com`, while `example.com` and `www.example.com` still do.

## Spec refs
- Review finding from 2026-04-07 against `src/config.rs:55`.

title: Derive managed zones correctly when DOMAIN_FILTER is unset
description:
## Outcome
When `DOMAIN_FILTER` is unset, create/update/delete operations derive the actual managed zone correctly instead of guessing from the last two DNS labels.

## Why this exists
The current fallback turns names like `app.example.co.uk` into `co.uk`, which breaks write operations for valid Njalla domains when operators rely on the documented default of managing all account domains.

## Scope
Replace the naive fallback in `WebhookHandler::extract_zone` with a zone-discovery strategy that works for multi-label public suffixes, ideally by matching against Njalla-owned domains and choosing the longest valid suffix. Add regression coverage for no-filter write paths.

## Non-goals
Do not introduce a broad public-suffix dependency if the Njalla domain list already provides the needed authority.

## Key implementation notes
The bug is in `src/webhook/handlers.rs`. Reuse the exact/subdomain boundary rules from the domain-filter fix rather than adding a second, subtly different matcher. Be careful about performance and caching if the implementation must query domains repeatedly.

## Dependencies / ordering
Depends on the domain-boundary task so the write-path lookup reuses the same exact/subdomain matching rules.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Leave `DOMAIN_FILTER` unset so the webhook must infer the managed zone.
2. Attempt a write for a host like `app.example.co.uk` while the account owns `example.co.uk`.
3. Observe that the current fallback derives `co.uk` instead of the owned zone.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Zone derivation uses owned-domain knowledge rather than guessing from the last two labels.
- Multi-label managed zones such as `example.co.uk` resolve correctly in write paths.
- Regression coverage exists for no-filter create, update, or delete flows that require zone resolution.

## Verification
- Unit tests: Multi-label suffix examples such as `app.example.co.uk` resolve to the owned zone rather than `co.uk`.
- Integration or e2e tests: Mocked no-filter create/update/delete flows derive the correct zone from Njalla-owned domains.
- Logging / observability / diagnostics: Zone-resolution failures remain explicit and actionable.
- Acceptance signal: With `DOMAIN_FILTER` unset, a write against a multi-label managed zone targets the owned zone instead of a guessed public suffix.

## Spec refs
- Review finding from 2026-04-07 against `src/webhook/handlers.rs:341`.

title: Align Nix healthchecks with exposed webhook routes
description:
## Outcome
The Nix-built container image and supporting project artifacts probe real health endpoints, so operators do not get false unhealthy signals from a healthy webhook.

## Why this exists
`flake.nix` currently configures the Docker image healthcheck to call `/health`, but the server exposes `/healthz` and `/ready`. That mismatch makes the shipped image look broken even when the app is fine.

## Scope
Fix the healthcheck path in `flake.nix` and review adjacent deployment/docs references touched by this bug so they remain consistent with the actual Axum routes.

## Non-goals
Do not redesign the health API or widen this into a general docs rewrite.

## Key implementation notes
Ground the change in the real routes in `src/webhook/routes.rs`. If multiple artifacts mention health endpoints, correct only the mismatches discovered by this review and keep the operator-facing guidance consistent.

## Dependencies / ordering
Independent of the runtime bug fixes.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Build the image from `flake.nix`.
2. Start the webhook and let the configured container healthcheck run.
3. Observe that the probe targets `/health`, which the service does not expose, so the container can report unhealthy even when the server is fine.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The Nix image healthcheck points at a real endpoint exposed by the webhook.
- Any nearby deployment or documentation references touched by this bug stay consistent with the actual routes.
- There is a verification path that would catch future drift back to `/health`.

## Verification
- Unit tests: Not expected unless a helper is introduced.
- Integration or e2e tests: If feasible, verify the built image or generated config probes `/healthz`; otherwise add a static validation check that prevents `/health` drift.
- Logging / observability / diagnostics: Health and readiness documentation clearly matches the routes the server actually exposes.
- Acceptance signal: The Nix Docker image healthcheck and related deployment references no longer point at a nonexistent `/health` endpoint.

## Spec refs
- Review finding from 2026-04-07 against `flake.nix:124` and `src/webhook/routes.rs:18`.
